responses.text: A May 2020 article in Decrypt claimed that quantum computers, supposedly developed in secrecy by governments, could crack Bitcoin's encryption "in just a few years' time."

The core of the prediction came from Andersen Cheng, CEO of Post-Quantum, who asserted there was evidence in the intelligence world that such decryption capabilities "could well be around two years away."
Post-Quantum is a commercial cybersecurity firm that actively builds, patents, and sells quantum-resistant security products.

As a vendor in the post-quantum cryptography market, the company and its CEO have a direct financial incentive to emphasize imminent quantum threats to drive adoption of their paid enterprise products.

Independent quantum researchers heavily dispute timelines of 2 to 5 years, explaining that overriding Bitcoin's elliptic curve cryptography with Shor's algorithm requires a fault-tolerant, large-scale "Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer" (CRQC).

Because of massive error-correction overhead, such a machine would currently require millions of physical qubits—orders of magnitude beyond the 54-qubit Google Sycamore processor cited in the article.
The 2022 deadline suggested by Cheng's 2020 prediction quietly passed without any quantum computer breaking Bitcoin's encryption, effectively invalidating the timeline.

Despite ongoing advances in quantum hardware, security experts and researchers maintain that no quantum system currently possesses the cryptographically relevant scale required to hack the Bitcoin network.
